one_liner: A 52-year-old woman presents with 3 months of progressive difficulty rising from a chair and combing her hair, along with a purplish rash over her eyelids and red papules on her knuckles.

stage2.hpi_extension: Over the past 3 months she has developed **symmetric proximal muscle weakness**  -  she struggles to **stand up from a seated position without pushing with her arms**, climb stairs, and **lift her arms above her head** to wash or brush her hair. The weakness is painless but progressive. At about the same time she noticed a **purplish discoloration of her upper eyelids** that her husband mentioned, and **reddish scaly bumps over the knuckles of both hands**. She has also developed cracked, darkened skin on the lateral aspects of her fingers and a rash across her upper back and shoulders that is worse after sun exposure. She reports mild dysphagia to solids. She denies distal weakness, sensory changes, or joint pains.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension; last mammogram 2 years ago, last colonoscopy never
stage2.social_history: Librarian. Non-smoker. No alcohol. No recent travel.
stage2.physical_exam: Skin: **violaceous (heliotrope) discoloration of the upper eyelids with periorbital edema**. **Erythematous papules over the MCP and PIP joints (Gottron papules)**. **Erythematous rash across the upper back and shoulders ('shawl sign')** and across the upper chest ('V-sign'). **Cracked, hyperkeratotic, darkened skin on the radial and palmar surfaces of the fingers ('mechanic's hands')**. Nailfold capillary dilation. MSK: **symmetric proximal muscle weakness**  -  shoulder abduction 3/5, hip flexion 3/5; distal strength 5/5. Reflexes preserved. Sensation intact. No joint swelling.

lab_results.result: **Creatine kinase (CK) 4,850 U/L** (markedly elevated). **Aldolase elevated**. AST and ALT mildly elevated (muscle origin). Creatinine normal. Electrolytes normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated CK and aldolase with transaminases of muscle origin confirm active skeletal muscle injury, as seen in inflammatory myopathies such as dermatomyositis and polymyositis.

lab_results.result: **ANA positive, 1:320**. **Anti-Jo-1 (anti-histidyl-tRNA synthetase) antibody positive**. Anti-Mi-2 positive. Anti-Scl-70 negative. Anti-centromere negative. RF negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Anti-Jo-1 is an anti-synthetase antibody associated with the anti-synthetase syndrome  -  myositis, mechanic's hands, Raynaud, interstitial lung disease, and arthritis  -  and supports the diagnosis of dermatomyositis.

lab_results.result: Left deltoid muscle biopsy: **perimysial inflammatory infiltrate with perifascicular atrophy**. Immunohistochemistry: CD4+ T cells and B cells in perimysial and perivascular distribution; deposition of membrane attack complex on capillary walls.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Perimysial inflammation with perifascicular atrophy and complement-mediated microangiopathy are characteristic histologic findings of **dermatomyositis**, distinguishing it from polymyositis (endomysial, CD8+ mediated).

stage5.final_clue: High-dose oral prednisone is started with gradual improvement in strength and CK. Methotrexate is added as a steroid-sparing agent. Because of the strong paraneoplastic association, age-appropriate cancer screening is pursued and reveals an early-stage ovarian carcinoma on pelvic imaging.

debrief.classic_presentation: Dermatomyositis presents in adults (peak 40-60, female predominance) and children as **symmetric proximal muscle weakness**  -  difficulty rising from a chair, climbing stairs, and lifting arms overhead  -  combined with characteristic skin findings. Pathognomonic cutaneous features include the **heliotrope rash** (violaceous eyelid discoloration with edema), **Gottron papules** (erythematous papules over MCPs and PIPs), the **shawl sign** (rash over the upper back and shoulders), the **V-sign** (upper chest), and **mechanic's hands**. Dysphagia, interstitial lung disease, and Raynaud may occur.
debrief.pathophysiology: Dermatomyositis is an autoimmune inflammatory myopathy driven by **humoral immunity and complement-mediated microangiopathy**. Deposition of the membrane attack complex on endomysial capillaries produces capillary dropout and ischemic injury, leading to the characteristic **perifascicular atrophy** seen on muscle biopsy with a **perimysial inflammatory infiltrate** (CD4+ T cells and B cells). There is a strong **paraneoplastic association**  -  especially with ovarian, lung, gastric, colorectal, and non-Hodgkin lymphoma  -  and adults require age-appropriate cancer screening.
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line therapy is **high-dose corticosteroids** (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) followed by slow taper. Steroid-sparing agents include **methotrexate**, azathioprine, or mycophenolate. Severe or refractory disease is treated with **IVIG** or rituximab. Topical sunscreens and hydroxychloroquine help cutaneous disease. **Age-appropriate malignancy screening** is essential at diagnosis given the strong paraneoplastic association.
high_yield_pearls: Proximal muscle weakness (difficulty rising from a chair, combing hair) + heliotrope rash + Gottron papules = dermatomyositis.
Elevated CK and aldolase reflect active muscle injury; anti-Jo-1 (anti-histidyl-tRNA synthetase) defines the anti-synthetase syndrome with mechanic's hands and ILD.

Muscle biopsy in DM: perimysial inflammation with perifascicular atrophy (humoral, complement-mediated). In polymyositis: endomysial inflammation with CD8+ T cells invading non-necrotic fibers (cell-mediated).
Dermatomyositis carries a strong paraneoplastic association  -  ovarian, lung, gastric, colorectal cancers, and non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Always screen for occult malignancy.
Treatment: high-dose steroids first; methotrexate/azathioprine as steroid-sparing; IVIG for refractory disease.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017)  -  Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Polymyositis / Dermatomyositis' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017)  -  Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Paraneoplastic syndromes  -  Dermatomyositis'.
